frameworks.name: resource-bounded / resource-rational cognition
frameworks.one_line: Cognition optimized under time, energy, and memory constraints

frameworks.name: inductive bias
frameworks.one_line: Hypothesis-space constraints needed before observing limited data

frameworks.name: approximation bias
frameworks.one_line: Inference and decision approximations under limited resources

frameworks.name: bounded rationality
frameworks.one_line: Errors reflect optimization under constraints, not irrationality

core_question: How can the brain be both highly capable and systematically error-prone under data and resource limits?
key_assumptions: Brains operate under fixed limits on time, energy, and memory
Inference requires constraining hypotheses prior to data
Decision making with limited resources requires approximations
Errors are consequences of constraint-optimal computation
key_findings: Human cognitive errors follow systematic, non-haphazard patterns
Characteristic errors emerge from systems built to meet data and resource constraints
interpretations: Inductive bias and approximation bias jointly explain the smart/stupid paradox of cognition
A unified computational logic links efficient inference to characteristic human errors
